items.title: Unit42: Understanding Current Threats to Kubernetes Environments
items.description: Palo Alto Networks Unit 42 explains that Kubernetes has become a prime target for attackers as its adoption accelerates in enterprise environments. Their research shows a sharp rise in Kubernetes-related malicious activity, driven less by classic container escape techniques and more by identity abuse and exposed application surfaces. Threat actors commonly gain initial access through misconfigurat
items.ai_summary: Threat actors are targeting Kubernetes environments through identity abuse and exposed application surfaces, rather than traditional container escape techniques. This allows them to gain initial access and potentially escalate privileges. To defend against this, it's crucial to implement proper configuration and access controls.
items.workflow_graph:
graph LR
    A([Threat Actor]):::actor -->|T1586.002| B[Initial Access]:::tactic
    B -->|T1611| C[Privilege Escalation]:::tactic
    C -->|T1649| D[Defense Evasion]:::tactic
    D -->|T1055| E[Collection]:::tactic
    classDef actor fill:#1a0e2e,stroke:#a78bfa,color:#c9d8e8,font-weight:bold
    classDef tactic fill:#0d2038,stroke:#4da6ff,color:#c9d8e8

items.title: TA416 resumes European government espionage campaigns
items.description: Since mid-2025, China-aligned threat actor TA416 has resumed targeting European government and diplomatic organizations after a two-year operational shift to Southeast Asia. The campaigns primarily focused on diplomatic missions to the EU and NATO, using web bug reconnaissance and malware delivery through compromised accounts and attacker-controlled infrastructure. In March 2026, TA416 expanded op
items.ai_summary: TA416, a China-aligned threat actor, has resumed targeting European government and diplomatic organizations through spearphishing attachment (T1598.002) and web bug reconnaissance, aiming to gain access to sensitive information. The campaigns primarily focus on diplomatic missions to the EU and NATO. To defend against this threat, organizations should prioritize email security and implement robust attachment scanning and filtering.
items.workflow_graph:
graph LR
    A([Threat Actor]):::actor -->|T1598.002| B[Initial Access]:::tactic
    B -->|T1059.001| C[Execution]:::tactic
    C -->|T1041| D[Command and Control]:::tactic
    D -->|T1486| E[Impact]:::tactic
    classDef actor fill:#1a0e2e,stroke:#a78bfa,color:#c9d8e8,font-weight:bold
    classDef tactic fill:#0d2038,stroke:#4da6ff,color:#c9d8e8

items.title: Detections for the Axios supply chain compromise
items.description: A supply chain attack targeting Axios npm package versions 1.14.1 and 0.30.4 introduced a malicious transitive dependency (plain-crypto-js@4.2.1) that executed during installation. The attack deploys cross-platform payloads across Linux, Windows, and macOS through a consistent pattern: Node.js spawns OS-native shells to retrieve and execute remote payloads in detached or hidden contexts. Linux vic
items.ai_summary: The Axios supply chain compromise is a technical attack where a malicious transitive dependency is introduced in npm package versions 1.14.1 and 0.30.4, allowing for cross-platform payload deployment. This attack has a real-world impact of executing remote payloads in detached or hidden contexts across Linux, Windows, and macOS. The most important defender action is to update Axios npm packages to versions outside the affected range.
items.workflow_graph:
graph LR
    A([Threat Actor]):::actor -->|T1195| B[Initial Access]:::tactic
    B -->|T1059.001| C[Execution]:::tactic
    C -->|T1041| D[Command and Control]:::tactic
    D -->|T1486| E[Impact]:::tactic
    classDef actor fill:#1a0e2e,stroke:#a78bfa,color:#c9d8e8,font-weight:bold
    classDef tactic fill:#0d2038,stroke:#4da6ff,color:#c9d8e8

items.title: Unmasking The 64-bit Variant of the Infamous Lumma Stealer
items.description: Gen Threat Labs has identified Remus, a new 64-bit infostealer attributed to the Lumma Stealer family, emerging after Lumma's takedown and the doxxing of its alleged core members. First campaigns date back to February 2026, with the malware switching from Steam/Telegram dead drop resolvers to EtherHiding and employing new anti-analysis checks. Remus shares multiple characteristics with Lumma inclu
items.ai_summary: Remus, a 64-bit infostealer, is a new variant of the Lumma Stealer family, employing anti-analysis checks and using EtherHiding for dead drop resolvers. This malware has been active since February 2026, with campaigns evading detection through virtualization and sandbox evasion techniques. To defend against Remus, prioritize implementing robust network monitoring and detection capabilities to identify suspicious web service interactions.
items.workflow_graph:
graph LR
    A([Threat Actor]):::actor -->|T1497| B[Defense Evasion]:::tactic
    B -->|T1102| C[Command and Control]:::tactic
    C -->|T1041| D[Execution]:::tactic
    D -->|T1082| E[Impact]:::tactic
    classDef actor fill:#1a0e2e,stroke:#a78bfa,color:#c9d8e8,font-weight:bold
    classDef tactic fill:#0d2038,stroke:#4da6ff,color:#c9d8e8

items.title: Leveling Up with NightSpire Ransomware
items.description: NightSpire ransomware, first discovered in February 2025, presents a categorization challenge regarding whether it operates as Ransomware-as-a-Service (RaaS). Analysis of two incidents from December 2025 and March 2026 reveals significant variations in tactics, techniques, and procedures between attacks. The March 2026 incident involved threat actors installing Chrome Remoting Desktop and AnyDesk
items.ai_summary: NightSpire ransomware poses a significant threat due to its evolving tactics, techniques, and procedures, with recent incidents involving the installation of remote desktop tools like Chrome Remoting Desktop and AnyDesk. The ransomware's ability to encrypt data for impact, as seen in the T1486 technique, underscores its potential for real-world harm. To defend against NightSpire, organizations should prioritize securing remote access points and monitoring for suspicious activity related to T1588 and T1486 techniques.
items.workflow_graph:
graph LR
    A([Threat Actor]):::actor -->|T1588| B[Obtain Capabilities]:::tactic
    B -->|T1486| C[Impact]:::tactic
    C -->|T1219| D[Exploitation for Client Execution]:::tactic
    D -->|T1071| E[Command and Control]:::tactic
    classDef actor fill:#1a0e2e,stroke:#a78bfa,color:#c9d8e8,font-weight:bold
    classDef tactic fill:#0d2038,stroke:#4da6ff,color:#c9d8e8

items.title: North Korea's Contagious Interview Campaign Spreads Across 5 Ecosystems, Delivering Staged RAT Payloads
items.description: A North Korean threat operation has published malicious packages across npm, PyPI, Go Modules, crates.io, and Packagist, impersonating legitimate developer tooling. The campaign uses GitHub aliases including golangorg and aokisasakidev to distribute staged malware loaders that contact actor-controlled infrastructure, retrieve payloads from Google Drive, and deliver platform-specific second-stage m
items.ai_summary: The North Korean threat operation compromises software supply chains by publishing malicious packages across multiple ecosystems, delivering staged RAT payloads. This campaign uses social engineering tactics to impersonate legitimate developer tooling, ultimately leading to remote access and potential data breaches. Defenders should prioritize verifying the authenticity of dependencies and monitoring for suspicious activity related to these packages.
items.workflow_graph:
graph LR
    A([Threat Actor]):::actor -->|T1195| B[Supply Chain Compromise]:::tactic
    B -->|T1219| C[Remote Access Software]:::tactic
    C -->|T1071| D[Command and Control]:::tactic
    D -->|T1082| E[Impact]:::tactic
    classDef actor fill:#1a0e2e,stroke:#a78bfa,color:#c9d8e8,font-weight:bold
    classDef tactic fill:#0d2038,stroke:#4da6ff,color:#c9d8e8

items.title: Threat Brief: Widespread Impact of the Axios Supply Chain Attack
items.description: A sophisticated supply chain attack compromised the Axios JavaScript library after threat actors hijacked an npm maintainer account, releasing malicious versions v1.14.1 and v0.30.4. These versions contained a hidden dependency called plain-crypto-js, which deployed a cross-platform remote access Trojan affecting Windows, macOS, and Linux systems. The malware performed reconnaissance, established
items.ai_summary: The Axios supply chain attack compromised the Axios JavaScript library via a hijacked npm maintainer account, releasing malicious versions with a hidden dependency that deployed a cross-platform remote access Trojan. This affected Windows, macOS, and Linux systems, allowing the malware to perform reconnaissance and establish remote access. Defenders should immediately update the Axios library to a trusted version.
items.workflow_graph:
graph LR
    A([Threat Actor]):::actor -->|T1195| B[Supply Chain Compromise]:::tactic
    B -->|T1219| C[Remote Access Software]:::tactic
    C -->|T1041| D[Command and Control]:::tactic
    D -->|T1486| E[Impact]:::tactic
    classDef actor fill:#1a0e2e,stroke:#a78bfa,color:#c9d8e8,font-weight:bold
    classDef tactic fill:#0d2038,stroke:#4da6ff,color:#c9d8e8

items.title: APT28 exploit routers to enable DNS hijacking operations
items.description: Russian cyber actors APT28 have been exploiting routers to overwrite Dynamic Host Configuration Protocol (DHCP)/Domain Name System (DNS) settings to redirect traffic through attacker-controlled DNS servers. Resulting malicious DNS resolutions enable adversary-in-the-middle (AitM) attacks that harvest passwords, OAuth tokens and other credentials for web and email related services. This puts organi
items.ai_summary: APT28 exploits routers to enable DNS hijacking, redirecting traffic through attacker-controlled DNS servers, enabling AitM attacks that harvest credentials. This puts organizations at risk of password, OAuth token, and other credential theft. Defenders should prioritize router security and monitor DNS traffic for suspicious activity.
items.workflow_graph:
graph LR
    A([Threat Actor]):::actor -->|T1557| B[Adversary-in-the-Middle]:::tactic
    B -->|T1583.002| C[DNS Server]:::tactic
    C -->|T1584.002| D[DNS Server]:::tactic
    D -->|T1557| E[Impact]:::tactic
    classDef actor fill:#1a0e2e,stroke:#a78bfa,color:#c9d8e8,font-weight:bold
    classDef tactic fill:#0d2038,stroke:#4da6ff,color:#c9d8e8

items.title: Bogus website fakes virus scan, installs Venom Stealer instead
items.description: A fake website impersonating Avast antivirus is tricking users into infecting their computers with Venom Stealer malware. The site runs a fake virus scan, claims to find threats, and prompts users to download a malicious file disguised as a system cleaner. The malware, identified as part of the Venom Stealer family, steals browser credentials, session cookies, cryptocurrency wallets, and other sen
items.ai_summary: The threat is a fake Avast antivirus website that tricks users into downloading Venom Stealer malware, which steals browser credentials, session cookies, and cryptocurrency wallets. This malware is spread through user execution of a malicious file disguised as a system cleaner. Defenders should block access to known malicious websites and educate users on the dangers of fake antivirus websites.
items.workflow_graph:
graph LR
    A([Threat Actor]):::actor -->|T1204| B[User Execution]:::tactic
    B -->|T1036| C[Masquerading]:::tactic
    C -->|T1555.003| D[Credentials from Web Browsers]:::tactic
    D -->|T1539| E[Steal Web Session Cookie]:::tactic
    classDef actor fill:#1a0e2e,stroke:#a78bfa,color:#c9d8e8,font-weight:bold
    classDef tactic fill:#0d2038,stroke:#4da6ff,color:#c9d8e8

items.title: Ringing in Chaos: How TeamPCP Weaponized the Telnyx Python SDK
items.description: TeamPCP uploaded malicious versions of the telnyx Python SDK to PyPI, compromising a package with 750,000 monthly downloads. The attack uses a three-stage architecture: a trojanized package triggers a platform-specific loader, which downloads a second-stage payload hidden in a WAV file using steganography, deploying a credential harvester. The harvester steals various credentials, encrypts them, a
items.ai_summary: TeamPCP compromised the Telnyx Python SDK on PyPI, using a three-stage architecture to deploy a credential harvester. The attack involves a trojanized package, a platform-specific loader, and steganography to hide a second-stage payload. Defenders should immediately update the Telnyx Python SDK and monitor for credential harvesting activity.
items.workflow_graph:
graph LR
    A([Threat Actor]):::actor -->|T1608| B[Stage Capabilities]:::tactic
    B -->|T1027| C[Obfuscated Files or Information]:::tactic
    C -->|T1059.001| D[Execution]:::tactic
    D -->|T1041| E[Command and Control]:::tactic
    E -->|T1486| F[Impact]:::tactic
    classDef actor fill:#1a0e2e,stroke:#a78bfa,color:#c9d8e8,font-weight:bold
    classDef tactic fill:#0d2038,stroke:#4da6ff,color:#c9d8e8

items.title: CVE-2026-2370: GitLab has remediated an issue in GitLab CE/EE affecting all versions from 14.3 ...
items.description: GitLab has remediated an issue in GitLab CE/EE affecting all versions from 14.3 before 18.8.7, 18.9 before 18.9.3, and 18.10 before 18.10.1 affecting Jira Connect installations that could have allowed an authenticated user with minimal workspace permissions to obtain installation credentials and impersonate the GitLab app due to improper authorization checks.
items.ai_summary: CVE-2026-2370 is a GitLab CE/EE vulnerability allowing authenticated users with minimal permissions to obtain installation credentials and impersonate the GitLab app. This issue affects Jira Connect installations and has a CVSS score of 8.1. Defenders should update to patched versions (14.3-18.8.7, 18.9-18.9.3, 18.10-18.10.1) to prevent exploitation.
items.workflow_graph:
graph LR

    classDef tactic fill:#0d2038,stroke:#4da6ff,color:#c9d8e8

items.title: CVE-2025-7741: Hardcoded Password Vulnerability have been found in CENTUM. Affected products co...
items.description:
Hardcoded Password Vulnerability have been found in CENTUM. Affected products contain a hardcoded password for the user account (PROG) used for CENTUM Authentication Mode within the system. Under the following conditions, there is a risk that an attacker could log in as the PROG user.

The default permission for the PROG users is S1 permission (equivalent to OFFUSER). Therefore, for properly permi
items.ai_summary: CVE-2025-7741 is a hardcoded password vulnerability in CENTUM, allowing attackers to log in as the PROG user with S1 permission. This vulnerability has a high severity score due to its potential for unauthorized access. Defenders should update CENTUM to remove the hardcoded password.

items.title: CVE-2026-5101: A vulnerability was identified in Totolink A3300R 17.0.0cu.557_b20221024. This a...
items.description: A vulnerability was identified in Totolink A3300R 17.0.0cu.557_b20221024. This affects the function setLanCfg of the file /cgi-bin/cstecgi.cgi of the component Parameter Handler. The manipulation of the argument lanIp leads to command injection. Remote exploitation of the attack is possible. The exploit is publicly available and might be used.
items.ai_summary: CVE-2026-5101 is a command injection vulnerability in Totolink A3300R's Parameter Handler, allowing remote exploitation. This publicly available exploit can be used to impact the device. Defenders should update the device to a patched version.
items.workflow_graph:
graph LR

items.title: CVE-2026-4176: Perl versions from 5.9.4 before 5.40.4-RC1, from 5.41.0 before 5.42.2-RC1, from ...
items.description:
Perl versions from 5.9.4 before 5.40.4-RC1, from 5.41.0 before 5.42.2-RC1, from 5.43.0 before 5.43.9 contain a vulnerable version of Compress::Raw::Zlib.

Compress::Raw::Zlib is included in the Perl package as a dual-life core module, and is vulnerable to CVE-2026-3381 due to a vendored version of zlib which has several vulnerabilities, including CVE-2026-27171. The bundled Compress::Raw::Zlib was
items.ai_summary: CVE-2026-4176 is a critical vulnerability in Perl versions 5.9.4 to 5.43.9 due to a vulnerable Compress::Raw::Zlib module. This module contains several vulnerabilities, including CVE-2026-27171, which can be exploited for remote code execution. Defenders should update Perl to a patched version to prevent exploitation.

items.title: CVE-2026-4946: Ghidra versions prior to 12.0.3 improperly process annotation directives embedde...
items.description: Ghidra versions prior to 12.0.3 improperly process annotation directives embedded in automatically extracted binary data, resulting in arbitrary command execution when an analyst interacts with the UI. Specifically, the @execute annotation (which is intended for trusted, user-authored comments) is also parsed in comments generated during auto-analysis (such as CFStrings in Mach-O binaries). This a
items.ai_summary: CVE-2026-4946 is a command execution vulnerability in Ghidra versions prior to 12.0.3, allowing analysts to execute arbitrary commands via UI interactions. This vulnerability is exploitable through improperly processed annotation directives in automatically extracted binary data. Defenders should update Ghidra to version 12.0.3 or later to mitigate this risk.

items.description: A critical security vulnerability in parisneo/lollms versions up to 2.2.0 allows any authenticated user to accept or reject friend requests belonging to other users. The `respond_request()` function in `backend/routers/friends.py` does not implement proper authorization checks, enabling Insecure Direct Object Reference (IDOR) attacks. items.title: A Little Bit Pivoting: What Web Shells are Attackers Looking for?, (Tue, Apr 7th)
items.description: Webshells remain a popular method for attackers to maintain persistence on a compromised web server. Many "arbitrary file write" and "remote code execution" vulnerabilities are used to drop small files on systems for later execution of additional payloads. The names of these files keep changing and are often chosen to "fit in" with other files. Webshells themselves are also often used by parasitic attacks to compromise a server. Sadly (?), attackers are not always selecting good passwords either. In some cases, webshells come with pre-set backdoor credentials, which may be overlooked by a less sophisticated attacker.

items.title: Russia Hacked Routers to Steal Microsoft Office Tokens
items.description: Hackers linked to Russia's military intelligence units are using known flaws in older Internet routers to mass harvest authentication tokens from Microsoft Office users, security experts warned today. items.description: When talking about credential security, the focus usually lands on breach prevention. items.title: Germany Doxes “UNKN,” Head of RU Ransomware Gangs REvil, GandCrab
items.description: An elusive hacker who went by the handle "UNKN" and ran the early Russian ransomware groups GandCrab and REvil now has a name and a face. Authorities in Germany say 31-year-old Russian Daniil Maksimovich Shchukin headed both cybercrime gangs and helped carry out at least 130 acts of computer sabotage and extortion against victims across the country between 2019 and 2021.

items.title: Feds Disrupt IoT Botnets Behind Huge DDoS Attacks
items.description: The U.S. Justice Department joined authorities in Canada and Germany in dismantling the online infrastructure behind four highly disruptive botnets that compromised more than three million hacked Internet of Things (IoT) devices, such as routers and web cameras. The feds say the four botnets -- named Aisuru, Kimwolf, JackSkid and Mossad -- are responsible for a series of recent record-smashing distributed denial-of-service (DDoS) attacks capable of knocking nearly any target offline.

items.title: Iran-Backed Hackers Claim Wiper Attack on Medtech Firm Stryker
items.description: A hacktivist group with links to Iran's intelligence agencies is claiming responsibility for a data-wiping attack against Stryker, a global medical technology company based in Michigan. News reports out of Ireland, Stryker's largest hub outside of the United States, said the company sent home more than 5,000 workers there today. Meanwhile, a voicemail message at Stryker's main U.S. headquarters says the company is currently experiencing a building emergency.

items.title: How AI Assistants are Moving the Security Goalposts
items.description: AI-based assistants or "agents" -- autonomous programs that have access to the user's computer, files, online services and can automate virtually any task -- are growing in popularity with developers and IT workers. But as so many eyebrow-raising headlines over the past few weeks have shown, these powerful and assertive new tools are rapidly shifting the security priorities for organizations, while blurring the lines between data and code, trusted co-worker and insider threat, ninja hacker and novice code jockey.

items.title: Who is the Kimwolf Botmaster “Dort”?
items.description: In early January 2026, KrebsOnSecurity revealed how a security researcher disclosed a vulnerability that was used to assemble Kimwolf, the world's largest and most disruptive botnet. Since then, the person in control of Kimwolf -- who goes by the handle "Dort" -- has coordinated a barrage of distributed denial-of-service (DDoS), doxing and email flooding attacks against the researcher and this author, and more recently caused a SWAT team to be sent to the researcher's home. This post examines what is knowable about Dort based on public information.

items.title: ‘Starkiller’ Phishing Service Proxies Real Login Pages, MFA
items.description: Most phishing websites are little more than static copies of login pages for popular online destinations, and they are often quickly taken down by anti-abuse activists and security firms. But a stealthy new phishing-as-a-service offering lets customers sidestep both of these pitfalls: It uses cleverly disguised links to load the target brand's real website, and then acts as a relay between the target and the legitimate site -- forwarding the victim's username, password and multi-factor authentication (MFA) code to the legitimate site and returning its responses.

items.title: Kimwolf Botnet Swamps Anonymity Network I2P
items.description: For the past week, the massive "Internet of Things" (IoT) botnet known as Kimwolf has been disrupting the The Invisible Internet Project (I2P), a decentralized, encrypted communications network designed to anonymize and secure online communications. I2P users started reporting disruptions in the network around the same time the Kimwolf botmasters began relying on it to evade takedown attempts against the botnet's control servers.
